name: Temple Mount
shortDescription: The Temple Mount, also known as Haram al-Sharif, is a hill in the Old City of Jerusalem, revered in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. It is considered the holiest site in Judaism and a significant site in the other Abrahamic religions.
description: The Temple Mount holds immense religious significance for Judaism, Christianity, and Islam, making it a focal point of faith and, historically, conflict. In Judaism, it is the site of the First and Second Temples, built by King Solomon and later rebuilt after the Babylonian exile. The First Temple housed the Ark of the Covenant, the most sacred object in Judaism. The Second Temple stood for centuries before being destroyed by the Romans in 70 CE. The Western Wall, a remnant of the Second Temple's retaining wall, is a place of prayer and pilgrimage for Jews. In Christianity, the Temple Mount is significant because Jesus visited and taught at the Second Temple. The New Testament recounts events that occurred there, including Jesus's expulsion of the merchants and money changers. In Islam, the Temple Mount, known as Haram al-Sharif (The Noble Sanctuary), is the site of the Dome of the Rock and the Al-Aqsa Mosque, two of Islam's holiest structures. Muslims believe that the Prophet Muhammad ascended to heaven from the Foundation Stone beneath the Dome of the Rock during his Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj). The Temple Mount's contested status stems from overlapping religious claims and political tensions. Its control and access have been sources of ongoing conflict between Israelis and Palestinians. The site's symbolism and spiritual importance make it a potent symbol for all three religions.

sources.title: The Jewish Study Bible
sources.author: Adele Berlin and Marc Zvi Brettler
